title: Ngā Rongoā o te Ngahere: The Forest Medicines of Ngāti Kahungunu / Ngā Rongoā o te Ngahere: Ngā Rongoā a Ngāti Kahungunu
subject: Te Reo Māori
culturalContext: This lesson is deeply rooted in the traditional healing practices of Ngāti Kahungunu, specifically focusing on the rongoā (medicinal) plants found along the Mangakōtukutuku Stream. Students will explore how their tūpuna (ancestors) identified, harvested, and prepared native plants like kawakawa, mānuka, and harakeke for healing purposes, connecting this knowledge to modern wellness practices.
learningObjectives: Identify and name at least 5 traditional rongoā plants in Te Reo Māori and describe their healing properties using simple sentences.
Recite a karakia for harvesting rongoā and explain its significance in respecting the natural world.
Create a simple rongoā preparation (e.g., kawakawa tea or balm) following traditional methods and present the process in Te Reo Māori.
Collaborate with peers to create a digital story or poster that shares the journey of a rongoā plant from the ngahere to the whare.

activities.title: He Hīkoi: Exploring Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku
activities.description: Students will embark on a guided hīkoi (walk) along the Mangakōtukutuku Stream with a local kaumatua. They will use a specially designed 'Rongoā Plant Identification Card' (in Te Reo Māori) to find, touch (with respect), and learn the names and uses of key plants like kawakawa (for digestion and skin), mānuka (for healing wounds), and harakeke (for binding and healing).

activities.title: He Wānanga: The Karakia of Harvesting
activities.description: Led by the kaumatua, students will learn and practice a specific karakia used by Ngāti Kahungunu when harvesting rongoā. This activity focuses on the correct pronunciation, rhythm, and deep meaning of the words, emphasising the values of whakamōmori (respect), manaaki (care), and kaitiakitanga (guardianship) for the environment.

activities.title: Ngā Mahi a Rongoā: Creating a Simple Medicine
activities.description: In small groups, students will follow a recipe in Te Reo Māori to create a simple rongoā. One group may infuse kawakawa leaves in hot water to make tea, while another may crush leaves with oil to create a balm. The focus is on following instructions, using measurement vocabulary (kotahi, e rua, etc.), and discussing the process in Te Reo.

activities.title: He Kōrero, He Whakaahua: Digital Storytelling
activities.description: Using iPads, students will work in pairs to create a short video or a photo collage with captions. They will tell the story of their chosen rongoā plant, from its identification on the hīkoi to the finished product. Their narrative must include at least five new vocabulary words and two full sentences in Te Reo Māori describing the plant's use.

resources: A local Ngāti Kahungunu Kaumatua (expert elder) knowledgeable in rongoā
The book 'Rongoā Māori: A Practical Guide to Traditional Māori Medicine' by Rob McGowan
Te Papa Tongarewa Online Collections - Rongoā Māori taonga (https://collections.tepapa.govt.nz/topic/3617)
Plant identification cards with photos and Te Reo Māori names/uses, specific to the Mangakōtukutuku area

tasks: Oral presentation: Each student presents their digital story or poster to the class, speaking for 1-2 minutes in Te Reo Māori about their rongoā.
Practical demonstration: Students demonstrate the process of making their rongoā, using the appropriate Te Reo Māori vocabulary for ingredients and actions.
Short written quiz: A matching exercise where students connect images of plants to their Te Reo Māori names and a short description of their use.

nzcAlignment: Te Reo Māori: Communication - Mana Reo (Using language, symbols, and texts to communicate and interpret the rongoā practices)
Te Reo Māori: Language - Mana Reo (Learning the specific vocabulary and sentence structures related to health and the environment)
Social Sciences: Place and Environment - Understand how people perceive and use places and environments (Tūrangawaewae)
Health and Physical Education: Personal Health and Physical Development - A3 Safety management (Understanding the safe and respectful use of rongoā)
